text: The video shows the darker pigmentation surrounding the anus, marking the outer border of the external anal sphincter. The location of the abscess is visible, with some spontaneous discharge present. The surgeon prepares to make an incision using either a knife or cautery, taking care to avoid damaging the circular fibers of the external anal sphincter by making the incision parallel to these fibers.

The surgeon performs a circumferential incision to drain a superficial perianal abscess, creating an oval extension to ensure proper drainage and prevent recurrence. A cruciform incision may also be used. The abscess cavity is gently explored for any remaining fluid collections.

The surgeon examines the ischorectal abscess, using small hemostats to open the abscess if necessary. Large bore needle aspiration may be employed if the collection is not easily found. Care is taken to avoid rigorous examination that could disrupt small arterial branches in the ischorectal space, potentially causing bleeding.
